statement: The EU's IUU Regulation (EC) No 1005/2008 establishes one of the world's most stringent systems to prevent illegal, unreported, and unregulated fishing. All marine fishery products imported into the EU must be accompanied by catch certificates validated by the flag state, certifying compliance with applicable fishing laws. From 10 January 2026, the digital CATCH system replaced paper-based certificates, making import controls more effective and harmonised. The EU can issue 'yellow cards' (warnings) and 'red cards' (trade bans) to countries with inadequate IUU controls. As a non-EU state, Iceland's fishery exports to the EU must comply with IUU certification. If Iceland joined the EU, its own domestic fisheries would fall under the full EU enforcement framework — including the revised Control Regulation's requirements for electronic monitoring, digital catch recording, and systematic cross-checking of landing data.
caveats: Iceland already has its own robust fisheries monitoring system. The question is whether EU membership would add meaningful enforcement capacity or primarily add bureaucratic requirements. Iceland has never received an IUU yellow card.

related_entries.statement: Noregur, sem EES-ríki utan ESB, hefur fullt forræði yfir sjávarútvegi sínum og semur árlega tvíhliða fiskveiðisamning við ESB um sameiginlega fiskstofna í Norðursjó og Norskuhafi. Þetta fyrirkomulag gefur Noregi sjálfstæði í sjávarútvegsstjórn samhliða samvinnu um sameiginlega stofna.

Sameiginleg sjávarútvegsstefna ESB (reglugerð 1380/2013) ákvarðar heildarafla í hafsvæðum sambandsins og skiptir kvóta milli aðildarríkja á grundvelli hlutfallslegrar stöðugleika, þar sem sögulegur afli frá 1973–1978 er grunnurinn að varanlegri hlutdeild hvers ríkis.

Hlutfall fiskistofna í Norðaustur-Atlantshafi sem veiðst er sjálfbært úr (við eða undir hámarkssjálfbærri nýtingu, MSY) hækkaði úr 5–10% árið 2003 í um 60% árið 2023. Miðjarðarhaf og Svartahaf eru þó enn alvarlega ofveitt, þar sem um 73% stofna voru yfir MSY-mörkum árið 2022.
